summary.brief: Elher v. Misra. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: Defendant Dwijen Misra, Jr., a general surgeon, clipped the wrong bile duct during plaintiff Paulette Elher’s laparoscopic gallbladder surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff’s expert, a general surgeon with extensive experience in the procedure, testified that clipping a patient’s common bile duct during an otherwise uncomplicated operation is a breach of the standard of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: Defendants’ expert opined that bile duct injuries frequently occur even absent professional negligence.
evidence_index.speaker: Defendant's expert

evidence_index.text: The trial court incorrectly applied MRE 702 and abused its discretion by excluding the testimony of plaintiff’s expert witness, Dr. Paul Priebe.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: Approximately nine weeks after the operation, Elher presented at a hospital with abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and jaundice.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Priebe, an associate professor of surgery at Case Western Reserve University, performs 50 to 80 laparoscopic gallbladder surgeries each year and has done so since learning the technique in 1990.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: Dr. Misra has performed approximately 3,000 to 5,000 such procedures and twice clipped the wrong duct, Elher’s surgery included.
evidence_index.speaker: Defendant

evidence_index.text: The trial court correctly concluded that the res ipsa loquitur doctrine does not apply to Elher’s medical malpractice claims.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: The manner in which a surgeon laparoscopically removes a gallbladder falls far outside the common knowledge of a layperson.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

initial_state.chief_complaint: Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and jaundice
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Approximately nine weeks after laparoscopic cholecystectomy, patient presented with symptoms indicating possible bile duct obstruction.

focused_exam.abdomen: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

requestables.name: ERCP
requestables.description: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography to assess bile duct obstruction

reveal.result_summary: Clip obstructing the common hepatic duct
reveal.clinical_significance: Confirms bile duct obstruction due to surgical clip
reveal.decision_impact: Necessitated surgical intervention to remove clip and reconstruct biliary drainage

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with symptoms of bile duct obstruction

key_events.event: Patient presents with abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and jaundice

timeline_phases.name: Diagnostic Workup
timeline_phases.description: ERCP performed to assess bile duct obstruction

key_events.event: ERCP reveals clip obstructing common hepatic duct
Common bile duct clipped during surgery

decision_points.prompt: What should the surgeon do to ensure correct identification of the cystic duct?

options.description: Convert to open procedure if anatomical structures are not clearly identified
options.clinical_reasoning: Ensures accurate identification of biliary anatomy, reducing risk of injury

options.description: Proceed with clipping based on laparoscopic view
options.clinical_reasoning: Risk of optical illusions leading to misidentification of ducts

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant proceeded with clipping based on laparoscopic view
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant stated that injuries can occur even with due care

expected_action_court.description: Convert to open procedure if anatomical structures are not clearly identified
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated this as standard practice to avoid duct injury

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to ensure clear identification of biliary anatomy led to duct injury
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Conversion to open procedure should have been considered
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient experienced complications from bile duct injury

key_findings: Court found that bile duct injuries can occur even with standard care
Expert testimony was excluded, affecting the plaintiff's case

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Use of intraoperative cholangiography could have prevented the injury

Bile duct obstruction due to surgical clip

Failure to convert to open procedure
Failure to refrain from clipping the common bile duct

court_findings.finding: Trial court incorrectly excluded expert testimony
court_findings.legal_significance: Case dismissed due to lack of admissible expert testimony

court_findings.finding: Bile duct injuries can occur even with standard care
